This is in response to your letter requesting advice

" a""o f"ia,Sldoorl and new trethics" provisions of the PoIiticaI

negotiations for future employmenta we are treating your Letter as
a request for infornal assistance.z
OUESTIONS
l.AsaninternwiththecaliforniacoastalComrnission,what
restrictions are imposed on your participation j-n Coastal.
comrnission matters relating to persons s/ith whom you may be

2- At the tine you terminate your employnent with the

CONCLUS IONS
1. As a designated enployee of the California coastal
commission, you are prohibited from making, participating in
making, or using your official 
position to influence, any
goverimental decision directly relating to any person v/ith whon
lou a.e negotiating, or have an arrangement concerning,
prospective emploYment.
2. You may not represent or act as agent or attorney for any
person before the california Coastal Comnrj'ssion for conpensation,
tor the purpose of influencing administrative action for one year.
In addition, you may not represent any other person before
the california coastal commission with respect to any federal
consistency review in which you monitored or participated' 
This
restriction has no time Iinrit.
FACTS
You have been an intern with the coastal Commission's Federal
Consistency Program since June, 1990. The Federal Consistency
program is- basei on the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act which
giv6s the california Coastal Commission Iimited regulatory
iuthority with respect to federal and federally permitted
activitils 
that affect the coastal zone. Under the Federal
coastal Zone Managenent Act, the coastal Commission may review
specified federaL licenses and permits v/hich affect Iand and
,.t"r, 
or affect the resources of the coastal zone directly or
indirectly through spiII-over impacts.
As an intern you are a designated ernployee required to.
complete an annual statement of economic interests' 
As an intern
you have been involved in the following:
l.Generalactivitiessuchastheproductionofane$,sletter
to ]ocal governments describing the proceedings of workshops
conducted by the Federal consistency Staff'
2. The monitoring of past federal- consistency proiects to
determine the success of mitigation measures imPlemented by the
coastal Commiss ion.
3. In addition you partj-cipate in lhe review of negative
determination statements and environmentaL impact statements'
when a project involves sites under the coastal Commission's
jurisaicti6n, 
the responsible entj,ty wi'll file a negative
ieterminatj,on statenent and a draft envj,ronnental irnpact statement
if they believe that the project wil'1 have only a de minir.nUs
inpact on the coastal Conmission's jurisdj'ction. 
Your duties
intlude comnunicating with the applicant and other involved
persons or agencies, drafting letters, preparation of a staff
report (with recommendations), and potentially presenting the

-366
Page 3
report to the coastal comrnission. You stated, however, that no
project in which you have been involved has proceeded to the point
of i staff report to the coastal Commission-
4. FinaIIy, between August and November 1990 you
participated in the preparation of comments on the Environmental
impact neport/ Envi ronmenta I fmpact Statement ("EIR/EIS") prepared
foi a proposed toII road in orange county by the san Joaquin HiLls
Transportation corridor (the "project"). 
The EIR/EIS was
puftiinea and circulated by the Transportation corridor Agency.
iou stated that you reviewed and annotated the EIR/EIS for review
by your supervisor. fn cooperation $/ith.your supervisor,- you then
wroie a draft version of coastal Comrnission comnents on the
project's EIR/EIS, which was reviewed, edited, revised and signed
^ny yorr supervisor. In addition, you handled inquiries from other
agencies ana the public on the document/ proj ect. or procedural
aipects concerning the comments and researched iurisdict j-onal
qulstions concerning the proiect should it continue' You stated
tnut tn" issuance oi Coastal Commission comments often depends on
staff rvorkload and are genera).Iy informal, with no substantive
bearing on any subsequent formal proceedings on the project' 
To
date, io formal application on the project has been initiated at
rhe coastal Cornmission.
You also stated that aIl the activities described above are
highly supervised. All public docunents and letters which you
neiped prepare are signed by your supervisor and any policy,
feqif, tr ttn"r matter on which you work is directed by, and
coordinated with, a supervisor. fn some cases, Iegal clearance
with the Coastal Comrnilsion's Iegal staff or coordination with
higher officers is required.
Recently you were notified that due to the california budget
emergency your-position nay be eliminated. consequently, you have
staried considering potential future employrnent. Two persons you
have considered conticting are involved in the project's EIR/EIS'
one is a private planning consultant who represents various
businessei, governmental agencies and public interest groups' The
ptanning consultant wrote comments on the project's EIR/EIS on
tehalf 6f clients in opposj.tion to the project- 
The planning
consultant may be involveO in the future in proceedings before the
coastal commission or litigation 
involving the coastal
Commission's decisions concerning the project.
Another potential employer is a Law firn representing
plaintiffs 
who are challenging the adequacy of the EIR for the
project. 
Again, with regards to the project, this.firm has had no
iir".t 
f orrnil involvement with the coastal Comrnission or i-ts
staff, except through informal comnents and inquiries. 
.However,
should formll federal consistency review by the Commission occur,
the firm could be representing clients in opposition to the
proj ect .

ANALYSIS
(1) Negotiates, vrithout significant
substantive review. with a governmental entity
or private person regarding the decj.sion; or
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to
the decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantj.ve review,
by:
(A) conducting research or rnaking
any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the
official 
or desi.gnated ernployee and the
purpose of whj.ch is to influence the
deci,sion; or
I. 
section 87407
Section 8740? provides:
No state administrative official, 
elected
state officer, or designated employee of the
Legislature shall make, participate in making, or
use his or her official 
position to influence, any
governmental decision directl.y relating to any
person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has
any arrangement concerning, prospective empJ-oynent.
You are a designated employee of the coastal Commission. As
such you are prohibited from making, participating j'n naking, or
using your official 
position to influence, any governmental
aeciiion directly relating to any person with !,hon you are
negotiating, or have any arrangement concerning, prospective
employment.
tihat constitutes "making, participatj'ng in naking, or using
official 
position to inf luencert is not defined in the new statute.
The Act does define "making, participating in making, and using
official position to influencet in the context of the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act. Regulation 18700 and 18700'1
prov ide :
A public official 
or designated employee
,'participates i,n the making of a governmental
dlcisioni' when, acting within the authority of his
or her position, he or she:


Our File NO. 
■-9■-366
Page 5
(B) Preparing or Presenting anY
report, analysis or opinion, oraLly or in
writing, which requires the exercise of
judgrnent on the part of the official 
or
designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.
Regulation 18700 (c) .
(a) With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official's 
agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
controL of his or her agency, the official 
is
attempting to use his or her official posj.tion to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, 
employee or consul'tant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include. but are not
rimitea to, appearances or contacts by the of f ic j'al
on behaLf of a business entity, client, or
customer.
(c) lJith regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the official 
is attempting to use
his or her official 
position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of inf I'uencing the
decision, the official 
acts or purports to act on
behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer, employee or
consultant of an agency. such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of of f ici-al stationery.
Regulation 18700. r (a)
and (c) .
Until def j.nj.tions are developed for Section 874o'7, Lhe
conflict-of-interest 
definition may be used as a guide. Pursuant
to this definition, a public official 
is using his or her official
position to influence a decision where the official 
contacts, or
lppears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, employee or consultant of the official's 
agency or any
agency appointed by or subiect to the budgetary control of .his or
frer agenty. (Regulation 18700.1.) Moreover, an official 
ls using
his or her official 
position to infLuence a decision before any
other agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decisj'on, the
official 
acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency. Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery. 
(Regulation
1,8700.1(c) . )


Our File No. I-91-366
page 6
Section 8740? does not define what constitutes 'rnegot iating, tr
or an rrarrangement" concerning prospective employment. However,
according to your facts, your question is prernised on the need to
seek and arrange nee, employment in the face of possible layoffs at
the coastal Commission. Thus, we assume you wiII be
"negotiating, " or making an I'arrangementrr concerning prospective
employment.
Hovrever, Section 87407 also does not specify when a decj-sion
is "directly related" to a person with whom an official 
is
negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective
employment. Until the Conmission has developed a regulatory
aefinition of "directly related to'r we are advising conservatively
that governmental decisions directly relate to a potential-
employer if the decision will- f inanci.ally affect the potential
employer, or if the employer has attempted to influence the agency
in any manner with respect to the decision. The two persons you
have described would appear to be sufficiently interested in the
project to trigger the restrictions of section a74o7.
II. 
Revolvino Door Restrictions Under the Ethics BiIl
Effective January l, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series
of statutes intended to establish ethical guj.deJ-ines for public
officials. 
The Act now provides that legislators, state
officeholders and designated employees of administrative agencies
are subiect to new restrictions on their post-emp Ioyr0ent
activity.3 
For designated employees of state agencies Section
87406 provides in relevant Part:
(d) (1) No designated employee of a state
administrative agency, any officer, employee, or
consul-tant of a state administrative agency who
holds a positlon which entails the making, or
participation in the making, of decisions which may
ioreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest, and no member of a state administrative
agency, for a period of one year after leavj.ng
of f j.ce or employnent, shall, for compensation, act
as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
any other person. by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employmen!, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose
of j,nf luenci.ng administrative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
These provisions do not apply to legislative staffers.


Our File No. 1-9■
-366
Page 7
permit, Iicense, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property. For purposes of
this paragraph, an appearance before a state
adninistrative agency does not include an
appearance in a court of law, before an
administrative 1a!, judge, or before the worker's
conpensation Appeals Board. The prohibition of
this paragraph shaII onJ.y appJ'y to designated
employees employed by a state administrative agency
on or after JanuarY 7, 1991.
***
(e) The Prohibitions contained in
subdivisions (b) , (c), and (d) shall' not apply to
any individual subject to this section vrho is or
becomes an officer or employee of another state
agency, board, or commission if the appearance or
c6mmunication is for the purpose of influencing
IegLslative or administrative action on behalf of
the state agency, board. or cornrnission.
You are a designated employee of the Coastal commission in a
position that your igency has determined entaj'ts the making or
iarticipating 
in making of aecisions which may affect a financial
lnterest. 
Pursuant to this section, you may not:
l. 
Act as representative, agent or attorney for any person
before the coastal commission or any other administrative agency
for which you have worked or rePresented during the 12 months
before Ieaving office or employment,
2. For compensation,
3. For the purpose of influencing administrative action or
any action or proceeding involving the. issuance, amendnent,
awirdj.ng, or rlvocation of a permit, Iicense, grant,.or contract,
or the iale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406')
"Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 as the
proposal, drafti.ng, developrnent, consideration, amendment,
Lnattrnent or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or
other action 1n any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-
Iegislative proceedi.ng. The prohibition continues for one year
afier you have ended your employment with the coastat comrnission'
(Section A7406- ) Hovrever, Section 87406(d) (1) exenpts appearances
in a court of IavJ; before an administrative law judge; and,
appearances before the worker's conpensation Appeals Board.
III. 
Traditional Revolvinq Door
However, even prior to the enactnent of the new ethics
provisions of the Act, the Act did irnpose a lifetirne restriction


on the activities of certain administrative officials 
under
certain circumstances. Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:
No former state administrative official, 
after
the termination of his or her employment or tern of
office, shall for compensation act as agent or
attorney for, or otherr+ise represent, any other
person lother than the state of california) before
lny court or state administrative aqency or any
of?icer or employee thereof by making any formal or
inforrnal appearance, or by rnaking any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence,
in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the followj'ng apply:
(a) The State of California is a party or
has a direct and substantial interest'
(b) The proceeding is one in which the
former state administrative of f icia I
part ic iPated .
section 87401.
No former state administrative official, 
after
the ternination of his or her employrnent or term of
office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist j.n representing any other person
(except the state of California) in any proceeding
in which the officiaL would be prohibited from aP-
pearing under Section 87401.
Section 87 402.
The California Coastal Comnission j.s a ,'state administrative
agency. " 
(Section 874o0(a) .)

f6aerif Consistency Staff of the california coastal Commission,

authority. a tnus, a-cording to Section 87400(b) you are a rrstate

basis, investigation or use of confidential

Iegal advisory opinions to depart:nental or agency
stirf 
whicn do not invol've a specific party or
Part ies .
clearly, generaJ. activities 
such as the production of a

Section 8740? does not define what constitutes 'rnegot iating, tr
or an rrarrangement" concerning prospective employment. However,
according to your facts, your question is prernised on the need to
seek and arrange nee, employment in the face of possible layoffs at
the coastal Commission. Thus, we assume you wiII be
"negotiating, " or making an I'arrangementrr concerning prospective
employment.
Hovrever, Section 87407 also does not specify when a decj-sion
is "directly related" to a person with whom an official 
is
negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective
employment. Until the Conmission has developed a regulatory
aefinition of "directly related to'r we are advising conservatively
that governmental decisions directly relate to a potential-
employer if the decision will- f inanci.ally affect the potential
employer, or if the employer has attempted to influence the agency
in any manner with respect to the decision. The two persons you
have described would appear to be sufficiently interested in the
project to trigger the restrictions of section a74o7.
II. 
Revolvino Door Restrictions Under the Ethics BiIl
Effective January l, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series
of statutes intended to establish ethical guj.deJ-ines for public
officials. 
The Act now provides that legislators, state
officeholders and designated employees of administrative agencies
are subiect to new restrictions on their post-emp Ioyr0ent
activity.3 
For designated employees of state agencies Section
87406 provides in relevant Part:
(d) (1) No designated employee of a state
administrative agency, any officer, employee, or
consul-tant of a state administrative agency who
holds a positlon which entails the making, or
participation in the making, of decisions which may
ioreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest, and no member of a state administrative
agency, for a period of one year after leavj.ng
of f j.ce or employnent, shall, for compensation, act
as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
any other person. by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employmen!, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose
of j,nf luenci.ng administrative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
These provisions do not apply to legislative staffers.
